video_title: Did The Bust Just Begin? | Jesse Felder
gist: Macro analyst Jesse Felder argues the AI capex boom is already metastasizing into a bust, driven by a physical inability to build data centers fast enough to absorb chip supply, collapsing hyperscaler free cash flow, and softening token demand under new usage-based pricing. He pairs this with a stagflation thesis and is bullish on energy and commodities as the contrarian hedge.

insights.claim: The AI capex boom is already transitioning to a bust, with data center delays through 2026 now turning into the first outright cancellations, which Felder reads as the writing on the wall before it shows up in price.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Front-runs analyst downgrades; signals to reduce semiconductor and AI-infrastructure exposure before estimate cuts manifest in price.
insights.quote: We've seen tons of delays cropping up. Now, we're starting to see the first cancellations. To me, that's that this is the writing on the wall.

insights.claim: Semiconductor stocks fell roughly 10% on Friday and last week was only the second or third week this year where more analyst price targets were cut than raised, an early sign analysts are beginning to capitulate on AI estimates.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Track the ratio of analyst price-target upgrades vs downgrades as a real-time gauge of the AI estimate-cut cycle turning negative.

insights.claim: A large data center operator put a 1 to 1.5 gigawatt project on hold this week at the request of the customer (speculated to be Microsoft), suggesting demand is being scaled back due to token-based pricing rather than build constraints.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Customer-initiated holds (vs supply delays) are the cleaner bearish signal that hyperscalers themselves see overbuild; watch capex guidance on next earnings calls.

insights.claim: Felder draws a direct analogy to the railroad bust: the boom ended the moment redundant routes started being cancelled, and AI is now at that exact cancellation inflection point.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Historical pattern says cancellations, not delays, mark the top; the first cancellations have now appeared.
insights.quote: as soon as they started cancing them, that's when the railroad boom literally came to an end and and boom turned to bust. So, I think we're right there.

themes.theme: Physics and Demand Constraints on Data Centers

insights.claim: Two-thirds of all data centers supposed to be completed in 2027 have not even broken ground, making it physically impossible to absorb the chip volume analysts have priced into semiconductor revenue.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Hard physical ceiling on chip deployment means forward semiconductor revenue estimates must be revised down regardless of demand narrative.
insights.quote: two-thirds of uh all the data centers that are supposed to be completed next year haven't even be broken ground yet.

insights.claim: Felder's single condition to abandon his bearish AI thesis is the physics of data center construction changing miraculously so multi-gigawatt facilities can be built far faster than the past few years have shown.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Defines a concrete, falsifiable invalidation point for the bear case: accelerated build timelines.

insights.claim: Nvidia ships GPUs to middlemen like Super Micro who build racks, but data centers aren't ready to accept the inventory, creating a backlog that strands suppliers in the value chain.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Inventory build at integrators like Super Micro is a leading indicator of channel stuffing; watch their inventory-to-sales ratios.

insights.claim: AI compute demand projections were inflated by a 'token maxing' period where venture capital subsidized usage to roughly one-tenth of true cost; the shift to per-token pricing by OpenAI, Anthropic and Microsoft is already causing demand to drop off.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: If demand was a VC-subsidized illusion, compute need has been structurally overestimated; rationing of usage undercuts data center buildout math.
insights.quote: We're only paying ultimately I think at onetenth or less of the true cost of AI.

insights.claim: On-device AI is eroding data center demand, with Apple selling many Mac minis that run agents fully locally and Dell and Nvidia developing on-device AI chips, reducing reliance on cloud compute.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: A structural demand leak from cloud to edge weakens the secular case for hyperscaler data center capex.

insights.claim: Sam Altman's underlying incentive is for the cost of compute to crash so OpenAI can run a profitable model, which Felder cites as a reason to distrust bullish gigawatt demand projections sourced from OpenAI.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Discount AI demand forecasts whose originators benefit from overbuild and falling compute costs.
insights.quote: It's in his best interest for there to be a crash in the cost of compute, right?

insights.claim: Token-request growth is being used as a key valuation metric for AI companies going public, creating an incentive to build bots that generate artificial token requests, a potential malinvestment or fraud vector.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: Treat token-request metrics in S-1s and pitch decks skeptically as potentially gamed; question demand quality.

insights.claim: The Magnificent Seven hyperscalers are losing their separate monopoly sandboxes (Google in search, Meta in social, Microsoft in software) and now all compete in the same capital-intensive AI arena, eroding their moats.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Convergence into one capital-intensive battleground compresses returns on capital; the moat premium in mega-cap multiples is at risk.

insights.claim: Free cash flow across the hyperscalers has gone to zero or negative because nearly a trillion dollars of AI spend this year exceeds their cash generation, forcing them to issue bonds to fund the buildout.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Rising hyperscaler bond issuance is a tell that AI capex is not self-funding; watch FCF and debt issuance as the second leg of the bust.
insights.quote: all of their free cash flow for all of these companies is gone zero or negative

insights.claim: Felder expects the second wave of the AI bust to be a realization that hyperscaler net income profitability is deteriorating as depreciation from prior capex ramps while revenue growth slows.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Even if semiconductor estimates cut first, the bigger market hit is mega-cap margin compression from depreciation; model rising D&A drag on EPS.

insights.claim: Citing Jeremy Grantham, Felder says the next decade for the hyperscalers will look so unlike the last decade as to be shocking, reframing the Magnificent Seven as the 'Maleficent Seven.'
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Challenges the core assumption underpinning index returns given mega-cap concentration; argues for reducing passive mega-cap exposure.

insights.claim: Felder compares current sell-side AI ratings to Henry Blodget during the dot-com bust, where analysts publicly rated companies highly while privately disparaging them, citing leaked internal Amazon and Google messages mocking the AI tools they're forced to use.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Internal employee skepticism at the implementers is a soft signal that near-term AI ROI is overstated.

themes.theme: Political and Local Backlash to Data Centers

insights.claim: Reno appears to be the first US locality to push through a data center moratorium amid backlash over rising electricity prices, signaling a bipartisan, nationwide political constraint on buildout beyond pure physics.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: Local moratoria add a regulatory cap on data center siting; watch for spread to other jurisdictions as a buildout headwind.
insights.quote: I believe this might be the first place in the country where they've actually just recently pushed through uh a a data center moratorum

insights.claim: Data center opposition is bipartisan and driven by both electricity cost spikes and air pollution from inefficient off-grid power generation operators install for their facilities.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Off-grid generation creates pollution-based political risk on top of cost; siting permits become a structural bottleneck.

insights.claim: The NFIB small business survey shows firms simultaneously raising prices and sharply cutting hiring plans, a leading indicator of both rising unemployment and rising prices, the definition of stagflation.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Use NFIB pricing/hiring divergence as an early stagflation read; supports defensive positioning and inflation hedges.

insights.claim: With CPI near 4% and core PCE coming in stronger, Felder expects rising commodity and oil prices to force the Fed to raise rates, pulling the punch bowl exactly as the AI bust hits.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: A hawkish Fed pivot concurrent with an AI sell-off is the painful scenario markets aren't pricing; raises tail risk for long-duration and equity.

insights.claim: Felder assigns a much higher probability than the market to a combined inflationary boom, higher interest rates, and AI bust occurring simultaneously, a confluence current positioning is unprepared for.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Households over-allocated to equities are positioned opposite this scenario; asymmetric payoff to hedging it.

insights.claim: The economy is unusually dependent on an AI-driven wealth effect plus hundreds of billions of dollars per quarter of direct capex injection, so an AI sell-off would hit the economy harder than the 2001-2003 dot-com bust did.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Unlike 2000, AI capex is a large share of GDP growth, so its reversal threatens recession, not just a sector drawdown.

insights.claim: Semiconductor stocks trade at the highest valuations in history with price-to-sales ratios off the chart for the sector.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Extreme sector valuation amplifies downside; a 40-50% correction is plausible from these levels.

insights.claim: More than 50% of stocks are now effectively caught up in the AI trade, including non-tech names like Ford and Caterpillar trading like AI stocks, making it nearly impossible to avoid equity pain if AI corrects.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Index and ETF investors have hidden AI beta; true diversification requires deliberately uncorrelated assets.

insights.claim: Because AI stocks are a far larger share of total market cap than dot-coms were and ETFs make the sector ubiquitous, a 40-50%+ AI correction would likely drag the broad market into a bear market unlike the contained 2000 bust.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: Concentration plus ETF ubiquity removes the diversification cushion that limited dot-com contagion.

insights.claim: Felder cites Warren Buffett calling markets more casino-like than ever in his lifetime and Berkshire Hathaway holding over $300 billion in cash as evidence, via actions, of extreme caution.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Berkshire's record cash hoard is a behavioral confirmation of late-cycle risk; follow the actions, not the words.
insights.quote: Warren Buffett is never going to come out and say... but by his actions, you know, he's saying this is this is a, you know, very dangerous kind of world

insights.claim: The market's speculative structure (heavy call-option buying, leveraged ETFs) resembles end-of-bubble conditions that historically end when the Fed removes the punch bowl due to inflation.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Elevated options and leverage usage raise the odds of a violent unwind once the Fed tightens.

insights.claim: Felder argues crude oil should be trading near $150 a barrel on fundamentals, with the current price suppressed by China importing ~5 million barrels/day less than normal and unprecedented strategic reserve drawdowns that can't last.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Identifies a large gap between suppressed spot oil and fundamental value; a catch-up move higher is the trade.
insights.quote: the fundamentals of the the energy um sector of the, you know, crude oil suggest crude oil should probably be trading $150 a barrel right now.

insights.claim: Felder uses gold as a leading indicator: gold's two-year rise foreshadows a roughly 20-month subsequent rise in the broader commodity complex, oil, and interest rates, now just beginning to be reflected.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Gold's prior breakout signals oil to $150 and the 10-year yield potentially to 6% over 6-12 months; positions ahead of the commodity/rates move.

insights.claim: The 10-year Treasury yield could rise to 6% over the next 6 to 12 months as commodity and oil prices break out and inflation worries push the long end higher.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: A 6% 10-year would pressure long-duration assets and equity multiples; argues for short duration and inflation protection.

insights.claim: Even if the Strait of Hormuz reopened today, experts say it would take 6-8 months to restore 80% of pre-war oil flow, supporting a sustained breakout in oil prices.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Supply recovery lags even on a ceasefire, so the oil supply shock has a long tail; supports the bullish energy thesis.

insights.claim: The energy sector is only 3% of the S&P 500 versus a historical 8-12%, the stocks are cheap and haven't priced in oil's ~50% YTD gain, and it trades contrarian to tech, making it Felder's preferred AI-collapse hedge.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Underweight, cheap, and inversely correlated to tech, energy is positioned to hedge an AI bust while benefiting from the commodity supercycle.
insights.quote: the energy sector is 3% of the S&P 500 and it's typically been closer to, you know, 8 10 12%.

insights.claim: Via the capital cycle framework, capital flooding into tech created oversupply while real assets and the real economy have been starved for years, setting up commodities for a multi-year supercycle.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Capital-cycle logic favors capital-starved real assets over crowded tech; allocate toward producers.

insights.claim: Felder is near-term cautious on precious metals despite the commodity bull case, citing silver's sharp outperformance of gold and surging gold-fund inflows that haven't yet reversed as signs of overheated speculation.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Wait for significant gold-fund outflows and capitulation, ideally near the end of Fed tightening, before re-entering precious metals.

insights.claim: Felder's central recommendation is diversification after a 15-year bear market in diversification, urging investors to add international stocks, real assets, fixed income, and cash rather than concentrated US tech.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Calls a regime change toward diversification paying off; rebalance away from concentrated US mega-cap tech.
insights.quote: we're probably at the cusp of a new bull market in diversification.

insights.claim: Within equities, Felder favors deeply out-of-favor defensive sectors, citing healthcare as interesting for value investors and consumer staples showing notable insider buying.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Rotate toward contrarian defensives; insider buying in staples is a bottom-up confirmation signal.

insights.claim: Felder warns consumer sentiment is at the lowest reading in the index's history (worse than the GFC and 9/11), yet says households are unprepared for a recession and bear market that could make conditions materially worse.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Record-low sentiment paired with high equity allocations signals complacency about downside; supports defensive repositioning.

insights.claim: Felder flags consumer stress with real income falling for consecutive months as inflation outpaces income and credit card defaults at multi-year highs, with housing on life support.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Bottom-of-the-K consumer weakness means any economic shock would compound quickly; watch credit defaults as a stress gauge.
